_comment: Daily Gambit — Word of the Day rotation pool. 90 chess vocabulary terms with plain-English Wife-Test definitions. /daily picks index = (dayOfYear % terms.length).

terms.definition: A position where any move you make hurts your own side. The player whose turn it is would rather pass — but in chess you must move.
When the player to move has no legal moves but isn't in check. The game ends in a draw. The most heartbreaking word in chess if you were about to win.

A special pawn capture — French for 'in passing.' If a pawn moves two squares forward past an enemy pawn's attack square, the enemy can capture it on the very next move as if it had only moved one square.
A two-piece move where the king and a rook switch positions, getting the king to safety. The only move where two pieces move at once.
When one piece attacks two enemy pieces at the same time. The opponent can only save one of them.
When a piece can't move because doing so would expose a more valuable piece behind it (often the king).
Like a pin in reverse — the valuable piece is in front and must move, exposing the less valuable piece behind it.
When you move one piece out of the way and a different piece, suddenly uncovered, gives check to the enemy king.
Check from two pieces at once. The only way to escape it is to move the king — you can't block both attackers.
Italian for 'little flank' — placing a bishop on the second square of a knight's file (g2, b2, g7, or b7) to point along the long diagonal.
A move that doesn't attack but quietly prevents the opponent's plan before it can begin. The art of seeing the threat before it happens.
A single move's worth of time. Gaining a tempo means making your opponent waste a move; losing a tempo means wasting your own.
An opening sacrifice — usually a pawn — given up early to gain a faster attack or better piece development. The Mind's Gambit takes its name from this idea.
The advantage of being the one who creates threats. The player with the initiative dictates what happens; the other side reacts.
A column with no pawns on it. Rooks love open files — they have the longest range and the freest movement there.
A column where your side has no pawn but the opponent still does. Often a target — your rook attacks the enemy pawn from a distance.
A square deep in enemy territory protected by one of your pawns, where an enemy pawn can no longer attack it. Perfect for parking a knight.
A pawn with no friendly pawns on the files next to it. It can't be defended by another pawn — both a weakness and, sometimes, a source of dynamic energy.
Two of your pawns stacked on the same file. They block each other and lose their teamwork.
A pawn with no enemy pawns in front of it or on the files next to it — nothing left to stop it from queening.
A pawn that has fallen behind its neighbors and can no longer advance safely. A long-term weakness.
When a pawn reaches the far side of the board, it transforms into any piece its player chooses — almost always a queen.
Promoting a pawn to a knight, rook, or bishop instead of a queen. Rare, but sometimes the only way to deliver mate or avoid stalemate.
Checkmate by a knight, where the enemy king is surrounded by its own pieces — no escape squares because its own army is in the way.
Checkmate on the back row, where the king is trapped by its own pawns and a heavy piece slides in for the kill.
When a player keeps checking the enemy king forever with no way to escape — leading to a draw by repetition.
If the same position appears three times with the same player to move, either side can claim a draw.
If fifty moves pass with no pawn move and no capture, the game can be claimed a draw.
When neither side has enough pieces left to deliver mate — for example, just kings, or a king and one knight. Automatic draw.
The first phase of a chess game — the first 10 to 15 moves, where both sides develop pieces and fight for the center.
The phase after development is finished, where plans are made and tactics explode. The most creative part of the game.
The final phase, when few pieces are left and the kings come out to fight. Where champions are made.
Moving your pieces toward the center, where they control more squares and reach more of the board.
Bringing your pieces off their starting squares into the game. The opening's main job.
The shape your pawns make on the board. Pawns rarely move backward, so the structure is the long-term skeleton that every plan must fit.
A diagonal line of pawns supporting each other. The base of the chain is the weakest link and often the target.
A short label from A00 to E99 used to classify every chess opening. Invented by a Yugoslav publisher in the 1960s as the world's chess shorthand.
The international chess federation — pronounced FEE-day — that governs world championships, official titles, and the global rating system.
Grandmaster — the highest chess title FIDE awards. Earning it usually takes a decade of serious study and three 'norm' performances at elite tournaments.
International Master — the title just below grandmaster. Strong enough to draw or beat most grandmasters on a good day.
Woman Grandmaster / Woman International Master — separate FIDE titles for women that follow the same norm system at slightly lower rating thresholds.
The rating system that puts a number on every player's strength. Invented by physics professor Arpad Elo. World champions sit above 2800; club players around 1500.
Over The Board — playing chess at a physical board, face-to-face, with an opponent. The original way.
Portable Game Notation — a plain-text format that records every move of a chess game. Like sheet music for chess.
Forsyth-Edwards Notation — a single line of text that captures any chess position. Each FEN is a snapshot of the board at one moment.
The modern way to write chess moves. Each square has a coordinate (a1 through h8), and each move tells you the piece and where it went.
Giving up material on purpose to gain something more valuable — usually an attack, a winning position, or a checkmate.
A sequence of forced moves with a clear goal — most often winning material or delivering mate. Chess at its most thrilling.
An exceptional move — usually a sacrifice — that solves a position in a way nobody saw coming. Old tournaments handed out brilliancy prizes.
A move that throws away the game — sometimes called a 'question-mark move' because annotators mark it with ??
A move that worsens your position but doesn't lose immediately. Marked with a single ? in annotation.
A move that isn't best but doesn't lose. Marked with ?! by annotators.
An opening pitfall that looks tempting but loses material or position if the opponent takes the bait.
German for 'in-between move.' A surprise move inserted before the move the opponent expects — often a check that changes everything.
German for 'air.' A space made for the king to escape back-rank threats — usually by pushing a pawn one square forward in front of it.
An attack through one piece to reach another. The attacker looks past the piece in front to threaten what's behind.
Two heavy pieces — two rooks, or a queen and a rook, or a queen and a bishop — lined up along the same file or diagonal to multiply firepower.
A bishop trapped behind its own pawns, with little to attack. A long-term problem in many positions.
A bishop with open diagonals and clear lanes — usually the bishop on the opposite color of your own pawns.
Having both bishops while the opponent has one bishop and one knight. In open positions, often worth half a pawn.
Each side has one bishop, but on different-colored squares. They can never attack each other — often leads to drawn endings.
When you push a smaller group of pawns forward against a bigger group on the opponent's side, hoping to create a weakness.
Two pawns standing side by side with no friendly pawns next to them. Strong on offense, fragile on defense.
A connected cluster of friendly pawns. Fewer pawn islands usually means a healthier pawn structure.
A square in your position that no pawn of yours can ever defend. Often a perfect home for an enemy piece.
A weak square deep in your half of the board. Enemy pieces sitting on a hole are very hard to dislodge.
Marching several pawns forward together against the enemy king, breaking up the cover in front of it.
A move that doesn't capture, check, or threaten anything obvious — but slowly improves your position. The hallmark of a strong player.
Arabic for 'army position.' A well-known starting position in a deeply analyzed opening, where the real game begins after dozens of memorized moves.
Often shortened to TN — a brand-new move played in an opening at the elite level, where everyone thought they already knew the answer.
Fast chess. Each player has between 3 and 5 minutes for the whole game.
Lightning chess — usually one minute per side for the entire game. Pure pattern recognition under pressure.
Quick but not crazy. Each player has 15 to 30 minutes per game. Long enough to think; short enough to feel the clock.
Long-format chess. Each player has 90 minutes or more per game. The world championship is played at this speed.
Extra seconds added to a player's clock after each move. Prevents games from being lost on time alone.
A pause before the clock starts ticking on each move. A less common form of time-control buffer.
Both players moving fast as their clocks run down. Where many otherwise won games are lost.
An old tournament practice: a long game would be paused overnight and resumed the next day. Killed off by chess engines, which made overnight analysis unfair.
A chess-playing computer program. Modern engines like Stockfish are stronger than every human who has ever lived.
The most famous open-source chess engine, made by volunteers around the world. The Mind's Gambit uses it for analysis.
Sending a chess position to a powerful server somewhere on the internet and getting back the engine's answer. Used everywhere from coaching to broadcasting.
A database of opening moves the engine has memorized so it doesn't have to think from move one.
A perfect look-up table for positions with seven or fewer pieces. Knows the exact outcome with optimal play.
A strong tournament performance that counts toward earning a FIDE title. Earning the grandmaster title usually requires three norms.
The lowest rating a player can fall to in a given system. Prevents extreme drops after a bad streak.
The set of openings a player has prepared and plays regularly with each color. Building one is a lifelong project.
The accumulated body of opening knowledge — books, databases, and engine analysis — that says which moves are best in known positions.
A new opening move that diverges from established theory. Top players spend months hunting them down before tournaments.
French for 'I adjust.' Said when touching a piece only to straighten it on its square — not to move it. Without this word, touching the piece commits you to move it.
The classical rule: if you touch a piece, you must move it. If you take your hand off after placing a piece on a square, that move is final.
Conceding the game without being checkmated. The polite way to end a hopelessly lost position — usually by tipping your king or shaking hands.
German for 'in-between move.' A move inserted into a forced sequence — usually a check or strong threat — that forces the opponent to respond before you continue the planned line.
A move that doesn't capture, check, or directly threaten anything, yet quietly improves your position. Often the hardest move to find in a tactical sequence.
A do-nothing move played to pass the turn back to the opponent. Useful when zugzwang or a structural weakness will force them to break their own position.
A piece left undefended where the opponent can capture it for free. Beginners hang pieces; grandmasters notice when others do.
A piece that is undefended or under-defended — not immediately losing, but vulnerable to a tactic. 'Loose pieces drop off' is a famous chess saying.
A piece with no safe square to move to. The opponent can capture it next move; defending it isn't enough if the trap is real.
A short, forcing sequence of moves — often involving a sacrifice — that wins material, delivers mate, or reaches a clearly better position.
Voluntarily giving up material to gain a more important advantage: better piece activity, exposed enemy king, decisive attack, or promotion.
Giving up a rook for a minor piece (knight or bishop) — usually for positional reasons: strong outpost, opened files, weakened enemy structure.
A tactic that lures an enemy piece (often the king) onto a bad square where it can be exploited. The bait is usually a sacrifice.
Forcing an opponent's piece to move away from a square it was defending, allowing a winning move on that square.
Inserting your piece between an enemy attacker and its target, cutting the line of defense. Often a sacrifice that wins the exchange.
Removing your own piece from a square so a more powerful unit can use it. A common motif in mating attacks.
Capturing or distracting the piece that protects a target. Once the defender is gone, the target falls.
A piece tasked with too many defensive duties at once. Attack what it can't protect simultaneously and something falls.
Two or more pieces lined up on the same rank, file, or diagonal — queen + rook, queen + bishop, two rooks — multiplying their combined pressure.
An indirect attack along a line where a piece 'sees through' another piece. The attack activates the moment the blocking piece moves.
A devastating sequence of alternating discovered checks and captures that lets one side strip away material round after round. Made famous by Carlos Torre vs. Lasker, 1925.
A classic bishop sacrifice on h7 (or h2) that drags the enemy king out into the open for a mating attack. Named after the Trojan horse.
The fastest possible checkmate — two moves for the side delivering it. Requires the loser to make the worst possible opening.
A four-move checkmate using queen and bishop to attack f7 (or f2). Common in scholastic chess; easily defended once seen.
Paul Morphy's 1858 game against Duke Karl and Count Isouard in a Paris opera house — a model of rapid development and attacking play taught to every beginner.
Anderssen vs. Kieseritzky, 1851 — a romantic-era masterpiece in which Anderssen sacrificed nearly all his major pieces to deliver mate.
Anderssen vs. Dufresne, 1852 — another Anderssen brilliancy, with a famous queen sacrifice. Called 'evergreen' for its lasting beauty.
A traditional award given at tournaments to the most beautiful game played. The award has shaped how generations of masters value attacking, creative chess.
A tournament format where players don't all play each other — instead, each round pairs players with similar scores. Used in big opens because it handles huge fields fairly.
A tournament format where every player plays every other player at least once. The fairest format, used for elite invitational events.
A tournament where losing eliminates you. The FIDE World Cup uses this format; one bad day and you're out.
The biennial team chess world championship organized by FIDE. Nearly every chess-playing country sends men's and women's teams to compete.
An eight-player tournament held every two years to determine the challenger for the World Championship match.
The classical match between the reigning World Champion and the challenger. Modern matches are best-of-12 to best-of-14 classical games.
Faster games (rapid, blitz, or armageddon) played to decide the result when a match or tournament ends tied at classical time control.
A final-tiebreak format. White gets more time but must win; Black gets less time but draws are scored as wins. No further draws possible — the war ends here.
Very fast chess — usually 1 minute per side for the entire game, sometimes with a small increment. Online platforms made it a mass sport.
Fast chess at 3 to 5 minutes per side. Quick enough to test instinct, slow enough to allow real ideas. The default speed of online chess.
Faster than classical but slower than blitz — typically 10 to 25 minutes per side. Many world titles are decided in rapid playoffs.
Long-form chess at 60 minutes or more per side. The format used for World Championship matches and most major tournaments.
Chess played over days or weeks per move, originally by post, now by email or specialized servers. Engines are usually allowed in modern correspondence play.
A simultaneous exhibition — one strong player faces many opponents at once, moving from board to board. A staple of chess outreach.
Playing without sight of the board, calling out moves from memory. Many grandmasters can play multiple blindfold games at the same time.
Also called Fischer Random — a variant invented by Bobby Fischer where the back-rank pieces are shuffled into one of 960 starting positions. Kills opening memorization.
A two-on-two variant played on two boards: when you capture a piece, your partner can drop it on their board. Chaotic, fast, and addictive.
A one-board variant where captured pieces become yours to drop back anywhere. Tactical chaos taken to its logical conclusion.
A variant where every capture causes an explosion that removes nearby pieces. Wildly different from standard play; popular online.
A sustained sequence of moves dragging the enemy king out into the open and chasing it to its death. The crowd favorite of attacking chess.
The protection around your king — typically pawn cover and defending pieces. Compromised king safety is the most common reason games end suddenly.
A game starting with 1.e4 e5. Tends to lead to fast piece play and tactical contests, the territory of romantic-era chess.
A 1.e4 opening where Black does not reply 1…e5 — Sicilian, French, Caro-Kann, Pirc, and others. Asymmetric and strategically rich.
A game starting with 1.d4 d5 (and similar setups). Slower piece play, deeper strategy, longer plans.
A school of opening play developed in the 1920s by Réti, Nimzowitsch, and others. Lets the opponent occupy the center, then attacks that center from a distance.
A famous rook endgame where the stronger side wins by building a 'bridge' to shelter the king from checks. Every endgame student learns it by heart.
A rook endgame defensive setup — the defender places the rook on the third rank to prevent the enemy king from advancing. Named for the 18th-century French master.
A defensive technique in rook endgames against a passed rook pawn — keeps the defending rook active. Named for Czech analyst Josef Vancura.
A famous study by Spanish priest Fernando Saavedra (1895) where promotion to a rook instead of a queen wins a king-and-pawn endgame. The textbook underpromotion problem.
In an endgame of king + bishop + rook pawn vs. king, if the bishop can't control the pawn's promotion square, the position is a draw — no matter how the stronger side plays.
A quick visual shortcut in king-and-pawn endgames: if the opposing king can step into a square drawn from the passed pawn to the promotion rank, the pawn can be caught.
A king maneuver in pawn endgames where one side spends an extra move by walking in a triangle, forcing the opponent into zugzwang.
When two kings face each other on the same file, rank, or diagonal with one square between them. The side not to move usually has the advantage — they can force the other king back.
Having both bishops in an open position. Considered a small but real advantage because the bishops control squares of both colors and complement each other.

Each side has one bishop, but they travel on different-colored squares and never meet. Endgames with this setup are often drawn even with material imbalance; middlegames with this setup can be sharply attacking.
Owning both of your bishops while the opponent has at most one. A long-term positional asset, particularly in open positions.
A bishop blocked by its own pawns on the same color is 'bad'; one that operates freely across open diagonals is 'good.' The difference often decides endgames.
The set of squares of one color (light or dark). A side that controls one color complex while the other side's bishop is the wrong color can dominate the game.
A square in enemy territory where one of your pawns supports a piece and the opponent has no pawn left that can drive it away. Knights love outposts.
A square that the opponent can occupy with a piece and you can't easily drive away — usually because the pawn that would have defended it has moved or vanished.
A particularly weak square inside your own pawn structure. The opponent can park a piece there permanently.
A connected group of pawns of one color. Fewer islands is better — your pawns defend each other and you have fewer weaknesses to defend.
An advancing pawn move designed to open lines for your pieces. Often the only way to free a cramped position.
A pawn move that offers a capture, forcing the opponent to either trade pawns or accept tension. A classic way to open files.
Advancing pawns on a flank where you have fewer than the opponent, hoping to create weaknesses in their pawn structure when they're forced to react.
An endgame situation where both sides push connected passed pawns toward promotion. Whoever queens first usually wins.
Two passed pawns on adjacent files. Together they are nearly unstoppable — much stronger than the sum of their parts.
Both rooks on the same rank or file with no piece between them. They defend each other and double their attacking power along a single line.
Moving a rook up off the back rank — usually to the third or fourth rank — so it can swing across to join an attack on the kingside.
An older term for chess opened by 1.e4 e5. Now mostly used to describe the resulting open, tactical structures rather than the move order itself.
The advantage of being the one creating threats. The player with the initiative dictates the game; the other side reacts. Closely tied to the concept of 'tempo.'
A coordinated arrangement of pieces around the enemy king that gradually closes off all escape squares before delivering the final blow.
A defensive resource where the losing side maneuvers into a position with no legal moves, claiming a draw. Vital in king-and-pawn endgames.
A classic mating pattern using a knight and rook (or queen) against a king on the edge of the board. Named for an 1803 German novel that featured the pattern.
A checkmate by two bishops on crossing diagonals, against a castled king. Named for English master Samuel Boden, who delivered it in an 1853 game in London.
An ancient mating pattern with rook and knight against a king in the corner. Records of it appear in Arabic chess manuscripts going back to the 9th century — hence the name.
An 18th-century mating trap in the open game involving a queen sacrifice, named for French player Sire de Légal who used it against an amateur in Paris.
A famous 1921 endgame study by Richard Réti where a king performs an apparent impossibility — chasing two pawns at once. A monument to the geometry of the chessboard.
An older tournament practice — when classical games ran over a long time, players could 'seal' a move and resume the next day. Largely extinct since the rise of fast time controls and engines.
In an adjourned game, the move written down in secret at the end of the day's play. The envelope is opened only when play resumes.
When a player's clock runs low and they must make many moves quickly. The classic source of last-minute disasters.
Running out of time on the chess clock. The literal meaning — in older mechanical clocks a small flag fell when time expired — has survived into the digital era.
A small amount of time added to your clock with every move you make. Designed to keep both sides from losing on time during long endgames.
An older time-control variation where each move you make gives back the time you used, up to a small maximum. Named for grandmaster David Bronstein, who proposed it.
The chaotic final seconds of a time-pressured game, when both players are slamming pieces in a race against the clock.
The traditional time-control checkpoint in classical chess. Reaching move 40 without losing on time has been an institution since the days of Capablanca.
A new player's rating before they have completed enough rated games for the rating system to consider it stable.
A number in the Elo formula that controls how much your rating changes after each result. Strong, established players have a low K; new and developing players have a high K.
A retrospective rating calculated from a single tournament's results. Often used to compare a player's tournament showing to their established level.
United States Chess Federation — the American national chess body. Membership is required for official rated play in U.S. tournaments.
English Chess Federation — the national chess body for England. One of the oldest in the world, descended from the British Chess Federation founded in 1904.
